script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for SOXX. Friday — here is your SOXX recap. The portfolio closed one point eight seven percent down on the session. A broad semiconductor selloff defined the day, and not a single major name escaped clean.

The chip sector took another hit Friday as geopolitical tensions and valuation concerns collided with a market already on edge. Applied Materials fell five point five seven percent — the steepest single-session decline in this portfolio — and the damage spread across the entire semiconductor equipment space without mercy.

The Nasdaq led losses among major indexes, dragged lower by a concentrated selloff in artificial intelligence and semiconductor names. Japan's tech weakness spilled into U.S. trading early, amplifying pressure across the sector. No major macro data print was available to counteract the move, leaving price action driven almost entirely by sentiment and geopolitical overhang.

Applied Materials was the headline number Friday, down five point five seven percent and the worst performer in this portfolio by a significant margin. The move came as chip stocks broadly sold off on geopolitical tensions and renewed valuation concerns, according to reporting from Yahoo Finance. Applied Materials, as a leading supplier of semiconductor manufacturing equipment, sits at the intersection of every macro risk currently weighing on the space — export controls, China exposure, and potential cuts to capital expenditure by major foundry customers. Seeking Alpha published analysis Friday suggesting the semiconductor ETF itself may be transitioning from a corrective phase into something more structurally bearish, even as Taiwan Semiconductor and ASML reported solid earnings. For this portfolio, the Applied Materials position at five point one eight percent of weight absorbed meaningful damage, and the pressure on equipment names appears unlikely to resolve quickly absent a clear policy signal.

Advanced Micro Devices was down one point zero three percent on the session. Yahoo Finance confirmed chip stocks tumbled broadly, with geopolitical tensions and valuation concerns cited as the primary catalysts. Attention now turns to upcoming earnings, with Advanced Micro Devices listed among the key names investors will be watching alongside major technology reports next week.

Micron Technology was down zero point five zero percent, the most resilient decline among the larger holdings. Micron crossed a notable threshold earlier in the week, closing below a one trillion dollar market capitalization level for the first time since early June, according to Dow Jones Market Data. Separately, reporting on India's smartphone market highlighted how the artificial intelligence memory crunch is reshaping consumer electronics demand — a dynamic that cuts both ways for Micron's near-term outlook.

Nvidia was down two point two one percent on the session. Apple briefly overtook Nvidia as the world's most valuable company by market capitalization Friday, according to Fox Business, as investors reassessed the outlook for artificial intelligence stocks and data center spending. That symbolic shift reflects a genuine recalibration in the market's confidence around artificial intelligence monetization timelines.

Broadcom was down zero point nine seven percent. Reporting confirmed the broader artificial intelligence selloff and Japan-driven weakness as the primary headwinds. Watch capital expenditure guidance from major cloud customers in the coming earnings cycle for the next directional signal.

Turning to Intel, shares were down two point zero zero percent on the session. Yahoo Finance noted the two percent decline specifically, placing Intel among the harder-hit names. Intel is scheduled to report earnings imminently, making the next few sessions particularly consequential for this position.

Over at KLA Corporation, shares fell three point zero two percent Friday. With no company-specific news, the move tracked the equipment sector lower alongside Applied Materials and Lam Research. KLA's exposure to wafer inspection demand ties it directly to any slowdown in foundry capital spending.

Marvell Technology was the lone bright spot in the portfolio, up zero point two zero percent. That marginal gain stands out given the severity of losses elsewhere and reflects some degree of relative resilience, though no company-specific catalyst was identified.

Lam Research was down two point three nine percent. Like KLA Corporation and Applied Materials, Lam Research faces the same equipment-sector headwinds — any reduction in foundry buildout directly pressures the order pipeline. Watch for commentary from Taiwan Semiconductor on capital expenditure plans.

Taiwan Semiconductor closed down two point seven seven percent, the second-worst decline in the portfolio after Applied Materials. Despite reporting solid earnings recently, the stock could not escape the sector-wide pressure. Geopolitical risk tied to cross-strait tensions remains the persistent overhang for this name.

Three data points to track heading into the next forty-eight hours. First, Alphabet reports earnings with capital expenditure guidance expected — that number will directly influence sentiment around artificial intelligence infrastructure spending and, by extension, the entire semiconductor supply chain. Second, Intel's earnings release is imminent and will be closely watched given the two percent decline Friday and the stock's position in this portfolio. Third, monitor any weekend developments on semiconductor export control policy, as geopolitical tension was cited explicitly as a primary driver of Friday's selloff and any escalation or de-escalation will set the tone for Monday's open.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for SOXX. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Watch Alphabet's capital expenditure guidance closely — it may be the single most important data point for the artificial intelligence trade heading into next week.
